x-enumeration-literals.name: Rollover Term Deposit
x-enumeration-literals.description:
Upon the maturity of a Certificate of Deposit (CD) or Fixed Deposit (FD), the bank automatically reinvests the principal (and sometimes the interest) into a new, similar term deposit.

Comment:
Examples: A 1-year Fixed Deposit that, upon reaching 12 months, automatically renews for another 1-year term at the current interest rate, unless the customer instructs the bank otherwise.

x-enumeration-literals.name: Rollover Loan
x-enumeration-literals.description:
A loan that is automatically renewed if not repaid in full by its maturity date. The remaining debt is carried over to a new loan contract.

Comment:
Examples:
Payday Loans: Short-term loans that, if unpaid, are extended to a new maturity date with additional fees.
Lombard Loans: Loans secured by collateral (like securities) that are rolled over continuously.
Rolling Credits/Revolving Credit: Credit facilities that automatically renew as they are paid off, such as credit cards.

x-enumeration-literals.name: Retirement Account Rollover
x-enumeration-literals.description: The transfer of funds from one tax-advantaged retirement plan to another (e.g., from an old employers 401(k) to a new 401(k) or an Individual Retirement Account (IRA)) without incurring immediate taxes.

x-enumeration-literals.name: Forex Rollover
x-enumeration-literals.description:
In currency trading, this is the process of holding an open position overnight. Because forex positions must be settled in two days, a rollover closes the position at the end of the day and simultaneously opens it for the next day, avoiding physical delivery of the currency.

Comment:
Examples: A trader holding a EUR/USD pair overnight will be credited or debited interest based on the interest rate difference between the Euro and the US Dollar

x-enumeration-literals.name: Rollover Mortgage
x-enumeration-literals.description:
A mortgage with a short-term maturity (e.g., 3, 6, or 12 months) that is automatically renewed at the prevailing interest rate

Comment:
Example: A 5-year mortgage consisting of annual tranches (1-year contracts) that are automatically rolled over at the new, current rate each year.
